chunk_summary: Management of post-traumatic and retained hardware infections involves prevention, surgical debridement, culture-directed antibiotics, and potential hardware removal, with therapy lasting 6-12 weeks or longer if necessary.
learning_objectives: Understand the distinction between post-traumatic osteomyelitis and hematogenous infections.
Learn the role of early surgical intervention and perioperative antibiotics in infection prevention.
Identify appropriate management strategies for early versus delayed hardware-associated infections.
Recognize the importance of prolonged therapy and counseling families about relapse risks.
key_facts: Post-traumatic osteomyelitis is commonly associated with open fractures, penetrating wounds, or postoperative contamination.
Prevention includes copious irrigation, aggressive debridement, stabilization, and timely perioperative antibiotics.
Early infections may allow for debridement and implant retention if the construct is stable, but delayed infections often require hardware removal.
Therapy duration is typically 6-12 weeks, extending longer if necrotic bone remains or implants cannot be removed.
Retained hardware increases relapse risk, and suppressive oral therapy may be considered in select cases.
reference_quote: Prevention remains fundamental: copious irrigation, aggressive debridement, stabilization, and timely perioperative antibiotics reduce later infection burden more effectively than delayed prolonged treatment. (Indian Academy of Pediatrics Consensus Statement)
document_key_topics: Role of surgical debridement and antibiotics
Prevention and counseling in bone infections
